content.value: Sequential circuits have memory: their output depends on both current inputs and past state. The basic memory element is the latch or flip-flop.

Built from cross-coupled NOR or NAND gates. S (set) forces output Q to 1, R (reset) forces Q to 0. When both S and R are inactive, the latch holds its previous state. Having S and R both active simultaneously is an invalid/undefined state and must be avoided.

A latch is level-triggered - it responds to inputs continuously while an enable signal is high. A flip-flop is edge-triggered - it only updates its state at the rising or falling edge of a clock signal, making timing far more predictable in larger synchronous systems.
The simplest, most widely used flip-flop. On the active clock edge, output Q simply takes on the value of input D. It is the building block of registers.
A refinement of the SR latch that eliminates the invalid state: when both J and K are 1, the output toggles instead of being undefined.
A toggle flip-flop: when T=1, output flips on each clock edge; when T=0, it holds. It's derived from a JK flip-flop by tying J and K together, and is the core building block of binary counters.

In a level-triggered JK latch, if both J and K remain 1 for the whole duration the clock is high, the output can toggle uncontrollably multiple times within a single clock pulse. This is solved using a master-slave flip-flop configuration, which only allows one controlled toggle per clock cycle.
Always state which edge (rising or falling) a flip-flop triggers on when drawing timing diagrams - this is one of the most commonly lost marks in DLD exams.

quiz.question: What is the key difference between a latch and a flip-flop?
options: Latches are edge-triggered, flip-flops are level-triggered
Latches are level-triggered, flip-flops are edge-triggered

quiz.question: In a JK flip-flop, what happens when J=K=1?
The race-around problem occurs in which type of circuit?
options: A level-triggered JK latch held with J=K=1 for too long
